submission_document.test_review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task presents a Go/C roguelike turn-based grid engine with intentional
bugs across multiple packages (visibility cache, effect synchronization, AI
planning, legality checking, resume-from-save logic). The agent must identify
and fix these bugs so that running the matrix script produces a clean resume
audit where fresh and restored execution paths produce identical, zero-illegal
move sequences for six scenarios. The test suite validates audit structure,
per-case correctness, wall collision rules, fog-of-war visibility, timed
effects, sidecar file ordering, and trace alignment.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 21-23)
Problem: pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are test-only dependencies
         installed in the Docker image. These should be installed in test.sh
         to maintain separation between the task environment and test harness.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Remove from Dockerfile and install in tests/test.sh instead:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/bin/bash                                                                │
│  apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl                                  │
│  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.7.13/install.sh | sh                    │
│  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                │
│  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       │
│  python -m pytest /tests/test_outputs.py -rA \                              │
│    --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json                                          │
│  ...                                                                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Test-only dependencies should not be in the agent's container
image. The agent is fixing Go code and has no use for pytest. Keeping test
deps in test.sh ensures the harness controls the test environment
independently of the task image.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Instruction Describes Behavior Not Covered by Tests
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 7)
Problem: The instruction states that "Running bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh
         <case> writes /app/output/single-<case>.json with the same top-level
         cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the
         matrix audit." No test verifies this single-case execution path.

Current approach: Tests only exercise run-matrix.sh and validate the full
audit. The one-case.sh script behavior is described as a requirement but
never verified.

Suggested fix: Add a test that runs one-case.sh and confirms its output
matches the corresponding row from the matrix audit:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_one_case_matches_matrix(matrix_data):                             │
│      """Single-case output must match matrix audit row."""                   │
│      proc = subprocess.run(                                                 │
│          ["bash", str(APP / "scripts" / "one-case.sh"), "rook"],            │
│          cwd=APP, text=True, capture_output=True, timeout=60)               │
│      assert proc.returncode == 0                                            │
│      single = json.loads(                                                   │
│          (APP / "output" / "single-rook.json").read_text())                 │
│      matrix_row = case_by_name(matrix_data, "rook")                         │
│      assert single["cases"][0]["fresh"] == matrix_row["fresh"]              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Every behavior described in the task instructions should be
verified by at least one test to ensure complete coverage and prevent
partial solutions from passing.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Could State the Agent's Objective More Explicitly
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 1-8)

Current approach: The instruction describes what a correct audit looks like
and what a clean run means, but never explicitly tells the agent that the
codebase contains bugs that need fixing. The agent must infer this by
running the matrix and observing failures.

Rationale: While acceptable for a "hard" task, adding one sentence such as
"The current codebase produces an incorrect audit; identify and fix the
issues so that the matrix run is clean" would improve clarity without
reducing difficulty, since the real challenge is locating and fixing the
bugs across multiple Go/C files.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-crafted hard debugging task that exercises deep code comprehension
across a multi-package Go codebase with C FFI. The seeded bugs span five
distinct subsystems and require understanding save/resume state management,
fog-of-war visibility, AI movement planning, and effect lifecycle logic.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Realistic multi-file debugging across Go packages and C code
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating — tests verify runtime behavior, not file contents
  ✓ Good test docstrings and comprehensive per-scenario validation
  ✓ Proper pinned dependencies and deterministic environment setup

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test-only dependencies (pytest) installed in Dockerfile rather than test.sh
  ✗ one-case.sh behavior described in instruction but untested

Evaluates: Multi-language debugging (Go/C), state machine comprehension,
           save/restore logic, fog-of-war algorithms

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move test dependencies from the Dockerfile into test.sh and either add a
  test for one-case.sh or remove its mention from the instructions.
================================================================================

Agent repeats full clean rebuilds more than three times without isolating which subsystem still diverges, -2

submission_document.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction explicitly covers the key behaviors that tests verify: the matrix command (`bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh`) writing `/app/output/resume-audit.json`; the `cases` array containing rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, warden; each case having name/ok/fresh/resume/moves/visibility/effects/records/log; fresh and resume summaries carrying illegal/steps/sig; the clean conditions (ok==true, illegal==0, sig match); records being a JSONL file mirroring the moves in order; log being whitespace-delimited rows in the same order; and `/app/bin/rogctl` being left in place. The instruction also describes the failure modes being fixed (blocking through walls, acting from unseen positions). Minor inconsistency: the instruction calls the actor field "side id" while the JSON uses `actor`, but the agent works with existing typed code and does not define the schema from scratch.
❌ fail - behavior_in_tests: The instruction explicitly specifies: "Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit." There is no test for this behavior — no invocation of one-case.sh, no check that single-<case>.json exists, and no comparison to the matrix audit row. This is a named, specified output with a defined correctness criterion that is entirely absent from the test suite.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are clearly named and each has a one-sentence docstring explaining what is being checked (e.g., "Baseline uninterrupted and restored paths stay clean for rook", "Sentinel timed modifiers must still be active immediately after restore"). Helper functions (assert_case_clean, load_layout, final_player_pos, lit_tiles, step_legal) are well-named and single-purpose. The session-scoped fixture `matrix_data` cleanly separates setup from individual checks. The structure is readable and easy to maintain.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the image, so the agent cannot read the tests or solution at runtime. The test fixture invokes the actual engine binary via subprocess and checks the resulting JSON, JSONL, and TSV files produced by the engine — there is no hardcoded expected output to match against. The solution itself works by patching buggy source files and recompiling, then running the real engine. There is no mutable external network resource (internet is disabled). The engine's correctness is verified end-to-end, not via string matching on pre-existing files.
❌ fail - structured_data_schema: The instruction does not document the exact JSON field names. It calls the actor field "side id" while the actual key is `actor` (used in tests as `move["actor"]`). "From coordinates, to coordinates" does not name the actual keys `from_x`, `from_y`, `to_x`, `to_y` that tests access directly. The `effects` array schema (fields `id`, `active`, `epoch`) is not documented at all, yet `test_effect_epoch_sentinel` accesses `row["id"]` and `ward[0]["active"]`. The log/trace file column layout (case_name\tturn\tactor\tfrom_x\tfrom_y\tto_x\tto_y\tlegal) is not specified — the test relies on column positions `parts[1]`, `parts[2]`, `parts[-1]` without any documented schema. Only descriptive prose ("whitespace-delimited rows") is given.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned by SHA-256 digest. All apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, golang-go=2:1.24~2, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Python packages are version-pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). The Go module has no external dependencies beyond the standard library.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Filenames are consistent throughout (resume-audit.json, single-<case>.json, run-matrix.sh, one-case.sh, rogctl). JSON field names in types, tests, and instruction are internally consistent. Script paths, Go package paths, and make targets all match their references.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies Makefile, go.mod, README.md, cmd/, config/, data/, docs/, internal/, probe/, and scripts/ into /app. Neither tests/ nor solution/ is copied into the image, so the agent cannot access them at runtime.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution patches four source files (b1/cache.go, c2/effect.go, d3/plan.go, probe/legality.c) with corrected implementations, applies targeted sed/Python string replacements to a0/pack.go and f5/run.go to fix specific logic bugs (Seen slice zeroing on pack, wrong resume-path loop structure), then runs `make build` to compile and `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` to produce the output. The solution derives the answer through an actual build-and-run process rather than emitting hardcoded JSON.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The instruction explicitly names all agent-facing output files: `/app/output/resume-audit.json` (produced by run-matrix.sh) and `/app/output/single-<case>.json` (produced by one-case.sh). The binary `/app/bin/rogctl` is also explicitly mentioned as being left in place. The records JSONL and trace TSV paths are not named in the instruction, but they are embedded inside the audit JSON output (via the `records` and `log` fields) and not files the agent is expected to name directly.
submission_document.test_quality_judge_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The test suite validates structural consistency and legality of moves but never
asserts that any moves are actually produced, allowing a trivial shortcut that
disables all hostile movement to pass every test.

Strengths:  Tests independently recompute expected visibility from layout files
and cross-reference move legality/coordinates against ground truth; the
sidecar-file tests verify structural alignment between output formats.

Weaknesses: No test asserts moves arrays are non-empty; all move-validation
loops are vacuous when moves is empty; the one-case.sh script described in the
instruction is never tested.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite runs the matrix build, checks that all six cases report ok=true
with matching signatures and zero illegal moves, then spot-checks wall
legality, visibility, effect persistence, and sidecar file alignment. However,
every move-based assertion iterates over case["moves"], which passes trivially
when that list is empty. An agent can disable hostile movement with a one-line
edit to PlanSlot (return false unconditionally), producing empty moves for
both fresh and resume paths, matching signatures, zero illegals, and empty
sidecar files — satisfying all 13 test assertions without fixing the actual
resume-state bug.

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. No minimum-moves assertion enables empty-output shortcut
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:112-136 (assert_case_clean)
Problem: The function validates moves only via a for-loop that never executes
when case["moves"] is an empty list. No assertion requires len(moves) > 0 for
any case. Since every scenario script contains at least one "H go" step that
should produce hostile movement, the tests should require non-empty output.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      if move["kind"] == "step":                                             │
│          assert step_legal(walls, ...)                                      │
│          assert (move["to_x"], move["to_y"]) in visible                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(case["moves"]) > 0, f"{case['name']} produced no moves"         │
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      ...                                                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An agent can make PlanSlot always return false (one-line edit in
internal/d3/plan.go), preventing any hostile from ever moving. Both fresh and
resume paths then produce zero moves, identical empty-list signatures, zero
illegals, and ok=true. All 13 tests pass without fixing the actual resume
state-restoration bugs in a0/pack.go and c2/effect.go.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Sidecar file tests are vacuous on empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:209-233 (test_sidecar_order_lancer,
         test_trace_sidecar_warden)
Problem: Both tests assert len(file_rows) == len(case["moves"]) then zip
over pairs. When both are zero-length, the equality passes and the zip loop
never executes. These tests provide no signal when the shortcut is applied.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) > 0, "records file must contain at least one row"        │
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without a minimum-length guard, an agent that zeroes out all
hostile movement produces empty sidecar files that satisfy len==len and
never enters the validation loop — the test is structurally vacuous.

================================================================================
                          WEAK ASSERTIONS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Signature match check is trivially satisfiable with empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:127, 178
Problem: The sig is computed via FNV-hash over the moves list. Two empty lists
hash to the same initial constant, so sig equality is guaranteed without any
actual move reconciliation occurring.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert case["fresh"]["sig"] == case["resume"]["sig"]                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert case["fresh"]["steps"] > 0                                          │
│  assert case["resume"]["steps"] > 0                                         │
│  assert case["fresh"]["sig"] == case["resume"]["sig"]                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The instruction specifically says "signatures match when both
paths ran opposing turns" — implying non-trivial turn execution. Without
requiring steps > 0, the test degenerates to comparing a constant with itself.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. one-case.sh functionality is completely untested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (no test exists)
Problem: The instruction states "Running bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>
writes /app/output/single-<case>.json with the same top-level cases wrapper
and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit." No test
exercises this script or validates its output against the matrix audit.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test exists)                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_one_case_matches_matrix(matrix_data):                             │
│      for name in CASE_NAMES:                                                │
│          proc = subprocess.run(                                              │
│              ["bash", str(APP / "scripts/one-case.sh"), name], ...)          │
│          single = json.loads(Path(f"/app/output/single-{name}.json")        │
│              .read_text())                                                   │
│          assert single["cases"][0] == case_by_name(matrix_data, name)       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a secondary constraint from the instruction that has no
coverage. An agent could break one-case.sh without detection.

================================================================================

[Container] 2026/05/25 15:57:03.627697 Running command cat quality-by-llm-comment.md
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction explicitly covers the key behaviors that tests verify: the matrix command (`bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh`) writing `/app/output/resume-audit.json`; the `cases` array containing rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, warden; each case having name/ok/fresh/resume/moves/visibility/effects/records/log; fresh and resume summaries carrying illegal/steps/sig; the clean conditions (ok==true, illegal==0, sig match); records being a JSONL file mirroring the moves in order; log being whitespace-delimited rows in the same order; and `/app/bin/rogctl` being left in place. The instruction also describes the failure modes being fixed (blocking through walls, acting from unseen positions). Minor inconsistency: the instruction calls the actor field "side id" while the JSON uses `actor`, but the agent works with existing typed code and does not define the schema from scratch.
❌ fail - behavior_in_tests: The instruction explicitly specifies: "Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit." There is no test for this behavior — no invocation of one-case.sh, no check that single-<case>.json exists, and no comparison to the matrix audit row. This is a named, specified output with a defined correctness criterion that is entirely absent from the test suite.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are clearly named and each has a one-sentence docstring explaining what is being checked (e.g., "Baseline uninterrupted and restored paths stay clean for rook", "Sentinel timed modifiers must still be active immediately after restore"). Helper functions (assert_case_clean, load_layout, final_player_pos, lit_tiles, step_legal) are well-named and single-purpose. The session-scoped fixture `matrix_data` cleanly separates setup from individual checks. The structure is readable and easy to maintain.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the image, so the agent cannot read the tests or solution at runtime. The test fixture invokes the actual engine binary via subprocess and checks the resulting JSON, JSONL, and TSV files produced by the engine — there is no hardcoded expected output to match against. The solution itself works by patching buggy source files and recompiling, then running the real engine. There is no mutable external network resource (internet is disabled). The engine's correctness is verified end-to-end, not via string matching on pre-existing files.
❌ fail - structured_data_schema: The instruction does not document the exact JSON field names. It calls the actor field "side id" while the actual key is `actor` (used in tests as `move["actor"]`). "From coordinates, to coordinates" does not name the actual keys `from_x`, `from_y`, `to_x`, `to_y` that tests access directly. The `effects` array schema (fields `id`, `active`, `epoch`) is not documented at all, yet `test_effect_epoch_sentinel` accesses `row["id"]` and `ward[0]["active"]`. The log/trace file column layout (case_name\tturn\tactor\tfrom_x\tfrom_y\tto_x\tto_y\tlegal) is not specified — the test relies on column positions `parts[1]`, `parts[2]`, `parts[-1]` without any documented schema. Only descriptive prose ("whitespace-delimited rows") is given.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned by SHA-256 digest. All apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, golang-go=2:1.24~2, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Python packages are version-pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). The Go module has no external dependencies beyond the standard library.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Filenames are consistent throughout (resume-audit.json, single-<case>.json, run-matrix.sh, one-case.sh, rogctl). JSON field names in types, tests, and instruction are internally consistent. Script paths, Go package paths, and make targets all match their references.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies Makefile, go.mod, README.md, cmd/, config/, data/, docs/, internal/, probe/, and scripts/ into /app. Neither tests/ nor solution/ is copied into the image, so the agent cannot access them at runtime.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution patches four source files (b1/cache.go, c2/effect.go, d3/plan.go, probe/legality.c) with corrected implementations, applies targeted sed/Python string replacements to a0/pack.go and f5/run.go to fix specific logic bugs (Seen slice zeroing on pack, wrong resume-path loop structure), then runs `make build` to compile and `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` to produce the output. The solution derives the answer through an actual build-and-run process rather than emitting hardcoded JSON.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The instruction explicitly names all agent-facing output files: `/app/output/resume-audit.json` (produced by run-matrix.sh) and `/app/output/single-<case>.json` (produced by one-case.sh). The binary `/app/bin/rogctl` is also explicitly mentioned as being left in place. The records JSONL and trace TSV paths are not named in the instruction, but they are embedded inside the audit JSON output (via the `records` and `log` fields) and not files the agent is expected to name directly.

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction explicitly covers the key behaviors that tests verify: the matrix command (`bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh`) writing `/app/output/resume-audit.json`; the `cases` array containing rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, warden; each case having name/ok/fresh/resume/moves/visibility/effects/records/log; fresh and resume summaries carrying illegal/steps/sig; the clean conditions (ok==true, illegal==0, sig match); records being a JSONL file mirroring the moves in order; log being whitespace-delimited rows in the same order; and `/app/bin/rogctl` being left in place. The instruction also describes the failure modes being fixed (blocking through walls, acting from unseen positions). Minor inconsistency: the instruction calls the actor field "side id" while the JSON uses `actor`, but the agent works with existing typed code and does not define the schema from scratch.
❌ fail - behavior_in_tests: The instruction explicitly specifies: "Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit." There is no test for this behavior — no invocation of one-case.sh, no check that single-<case>.json exists, and no comparison to the matrix audit row. This is a named, specified output with a defined correctness criterion that is entirely absent from the test suite.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are clearly named and each has a one-sentence docstring explaining what is being checked (e.g., "Baseline uninterrupted and restored paths stay clean for rook", "Sentinel timed modifiers must still be active immediately after restore"). Helper functions (assert_case_clean, load_layout, final_player_pos, lit_tiles, step_legal) are well-named and single-purpose. The session-scoped fixture `matrix_data` cleanly separates setup from individual checks. The structure is readable and easy to maintain.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the image, so the agent cannot read the tests or solution at runtime. The test fixture invokes the actual engine binary via subprocess and checks the resulting JSON, JSONL, and TSV files produced by the engine — there is no hardcoded expected output to match against. The solution itself works by patching buggy source files and recompiling, then running the real engine. There is no mutable external network resource (internet is disabled). The engine's correctness is verified end-to-end, not via string matching on pre-existing files.
❌ fail - structured_data_schema: The instruction does not document the exact JSON field names. It calls the actor field "side id" while the actual key is `actor` (used in tests as `move["actor"]`). "From coordinates, to coordinates" does not name the actual keys `from_x`, `from_y`, `to_x`, `to_y` that tests access directly. The `effects` array schema (fields `id`, `active`, `epoch`) is not documented at all, yet `test_effect_epoch_sentinel` accesses `row["id"]` and `ward[0]["active"]`. The log/trace file column layout (case_name\tturn\tactor\tfrom_x\tfrom_y\tto_x\tto_y\tlegal) is not specified — the test relies on column positions `parts[1]`, `parts[2]`, `parts[-1]` without any documented schema. Only descriptive prose ("whitespace-delimited rows") is given.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned by SHA-256 digest. All apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, golang-go=2:1.24~2, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Python packages are version-pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). The Go module has no external dependencies beyond the standard library.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Filenames are consistent throughout (resume-audit.json, single-<case>.json, run-matrix.sh, one-case.sh, rogctl). JSON field names in types, tests, and instruction are internally consistent. Script paths, Go package paths, and make targets all match their references.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies Makefile, go.mod, README.md, cmd/, config/, data/, docs/, internal/, probe/, and scripts/ into /app. Neither tests/ nor solution/ is copied into the image, so the agent cannot access them at runtime.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution patches four source files (b1/cache.go, c2/effect.go, d3/plan.go, probe/legality.c) with corrected implementations, applies targeted sed/Python string replacements to a0/pack.go and f5/run.go to fix specific logic bugs (Seen slice zeroing on pack, wrong resume-path loop structure), then runs `make build` to compile and `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` to produce the output. The solution derives the answer through an actual build-and-run process rather than emitting hardcoded JSON.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The instruction explicitly names all agent-facing output files: `/app/output/resume-audit.json` (produced by run-matrix.sh) and `/app/output/single-<case>.json` (produced by one-case.sh). The binary `/app/bin/rogctl` is also explicitly mentioned as being left in place. The records JSONL and trace TSV paths are not named in the instruction, but they are embedded inside the audit JSON output (via the `records` and `log` fields) and not files the agent is expected to name directly.

[Container] 2026/05/25 15:53:03.365664 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.

2026-05-25 15:55:35,548 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (29 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The test suite validates structural consistency and legality of moves but never
asserts that any moves are actually produced, allowing a trivial shortcut that
disables all hostile movement to pass every test.

Strengths:  Tests independently recompute expected visibility from layout files
and cross-reference move legality/coordinates against ground truth; the
sidecar-file tests verify structural alignment between output formats.

Weaknesses: No test asserts moves arrays are non-empty; all move-validation
loops are vacuous when moves is empty; the one-case.sh script described in the
instruction is never tested.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite runs the matrix build, checks that all six cases report ok=true
with matching signatures and zero illegal moves, then spot-checks wall
legality, visibility, effect persistence, and sidecar file alignment. However,
every move-based assertion iterates over case["moves"], which passes trivially
when that list is empty. An agent can disable hostile movement with a one-line
edit to PlanSlot (return false unconditionally), producing empty moves for
both fresh and resume paths, matching signatures, zero illegals, and empty
sidecar files — satisfying all 13 test assertions without fixing the actual
resume-state bug.

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. No minimum-moves assertion enables empty-output shortcut
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:112-136 (assert_case_clean)
Problem: The function validates moves only via a for-loop that never executes
when case["moves"] is an empty list. No assertion requires len(moves) > 0 for
any case. Since every scenario script contains at least one "H go" step that
should produce hostile movement, the tests should require non-empty output.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      if move["kind"] == "step":                                             │
│          assert step_legal(walls, ...)                                      │
│          assert (move["to_x"], move["to_y"]) in visible                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(case["moves"]) > 0, f"{case['name']} produced no moves"         │
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      ...                                                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An agent can make PlanSlot always return false (one-line edit in
internal/d3/plan.go), preventing any hostile from ever moving. Both fresh and
resume paths then produce zero moves, identical empty-list signatures, zero
illegals, and ok=true. All 13 tests pass without fixing the actual resume
state-restoration bugs in a0/pack.go and c2/effect.go.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Sidecar file tests are vacuous on empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:209-233 (test_sidecar_order_lancer,
         test_trace_sidecar_warden)
Problem: Both tests assert len(file_rows) == len(case["moves"]) then zip
over pairs. When both are zero-length, the equality passes and the zip loop
never executes. These tests provide no signal when the shortcut is applied.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) > 0, "records file must contain at least one row"        │
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without a minimum-length guard, an agent that zeroes out all
hostile movement produces empty sidecar files that satisfy len==len and
never enters the validation loop — the test is structurally vacuous.

================================================================================
                          WEAK ASSERTIONS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Signature match check is trivially satisfiable with empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:127, 178
Problem: The sig is computed via FNV-hash over the moves list. Two empty lists
hash to the same initial constant, so sig equality is guaranteed without any
actual move reconciliation occurring.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert case["fresh"]["sig"] == case["resume"]["sig"]                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert case["fresh"]["steps"] > 0                                          │
│  assert case["resume"]["steps"] > 0                                         │
│  assert case["fresh"]["sig"] == case["resume"]["sig"]                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The instruction specifically says "signatures match when both
paths ran opposing turns" — implying non-trivial turn execution. Without
requiring steps > 0, the test degenerates to comparing a constant with itself.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. one-case.sh functionality is completely untested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (no test exists)
Problem: The instruction states "Running bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>
writes /app/output/single-<case>.json with the same top-level cases wrapper
and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit." No test
exercises this script or validates its output against the matrix audit.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test exists)                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_one_case_matches_matrix(matrix_data):                             │
│      for name in CASE_NAMES:                                                │
│          proc = subprocess.run(                                              │
│              ["bash", str(APP / "scripts/one-case.sh"), name], ...)          │
│          single = json.loads(Path(f"/app/output/single-{name}.json")        │
│              .read_text())                                                   │
│          assert single["cases"][0] == case_by_name(matrix_data, name)       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a secondary constraint from the instruction that has no
coverage. An agent could break one-case.sh without detection.

================================================================================

The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.

Agent fixes internal/c2/effect.go SyncBoundary to correctly remap effect expiry turns based on the time delta between saved and current turns, +3
Agent fixes internal/d3/plan.go to remove the incorrect cache-based visibility override in PlanSlot and correct the hostile movement planning logic, +3
Agent fixes probe/legality.c walk_e to properly enforce Manhattan distance == 1 for legal steps and adds bounds checking, +2
Agent fixes the runLoaded function in internal/f5/run.go to properly replay post-save player moves and hostile turns instead of only advancing on save action steps, +5

Reviewing task: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task (skill: terminus-2nd-ed-task-reviewer)
Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task presents a Go/C roguelike turn-based grid engine with intentional
bugs across multiple packages (visibility cache, effect synchronization, AI
planning, legality checking, resume-from-save logic). The agent must identify
and fix these bugs so that running the matrix script produces a clean resume
audit where fresh and restored execution paths produce identical, zero-illegal
move sequences for six scenarios. The test suite validates audit structure,
per-case correctness, wall collision rules, fog-of-war visibility, timed
effects, sidecar file ordering, and trace alignment.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 21-23)
Problem: pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are test-only dependencies
         installed in the Docker image. These should be installed in test.sh
         to maintain separation between the task environment and test harness.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Remove from Dockerfile and install in tests/test.sh instead:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/bin/bash                                                                │
│  apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl                                  │
│  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.7.13/install.sh | sh                    │
│  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                │
│  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       │
│  python -m pytest /tests/test_outputs.py -rA \                              │
│    --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json                                          │
│  ...                                                                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Test-only dependencies should not be in the agent's container
image. The agent is fixing Go code and has no use for pytest. Keeping test
deps in test.sh ensures the harness controls the test environment
independently of the task image.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Instruction Describes Behavior Not Covered by Tests
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 7)
Problem: The instruction states that "Running bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh
         <case> writes /app/output/single-<case>.json with the same top-level
         cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the
         matrix audit." No test verifies this single-case execution path.

Current approach: Tests only exercise run-matrix.sh and validate the full
audit. The one-case.sh script behavior is described as a requirement but
never verified.

Suggested fix: Add a test that runs one-case.sh and confirms its output
matches the corresponding row from the matrix audit:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_one_case_matches_matrix(matrix_data):                             │
│      """Single-case output must match matrix audit row."""                   │
│      proc = subprocess.run(                                                 │
│          ["bash", str(APP / "scripts" / "one-case.sh"), "rook"],            │
│          cwd=APP, text=True, capture_output=True, timeout=60)               │
│      assert proc.returncode == 0                                            │
│      single = json.loads(                                                   │
│          (APP / "output" / "single-rook.json").read_text())                 │
│      matrix_row = case_by_name(matrix_data, "rook")                         │
│      assert single["cases"][0]["fresh"] == matrix_row["fresh"]              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Every behavior described in the task instructions should be
verified by at least one test to ensure complete coverage and prevent
partial solutions from passing.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Could State the Agent's Objective More Explicitly
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 1-8)

Current approach: The instruction describes what a correct audit looks like
and what a clean run means, but never explicitly tells the agent that the
codebase contains bugs that need fixing. The agent must infer this by
running the matrix and observing failures.

Rationale: While acceptable for a "hard" task, adding one sentence such as
"The current codebase produces an incorrect audit; identify and fix the
issues so that the matrix run is clean" would improve clarity without
reducing difficulty, since the real challenge is locating and fixing the
bugs across multiple Go/C files.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-crafted hard debugging task that exercises deep code comprehension
across a multi-package Go codebase with C FFI. The seeded bugs span five
distinct subsystems and require understanding save/resume state management,
fog-of-war visibility, AI movement planning, and effect lifecycle logic.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Realistic multi-file debugging across Go packages and C code
  ✓ Strong anti-cheating — tests verify runtime behavior, not file contents
  ✓ Good test docstrings and comprehensive per-scenario validation
  ✓ Proper pinned dependencies and deterministic environment setup

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test-only dependencies (pytest) installed in Dockerfile rather than test.sh
  ✗ one-case.sh behavior described in instruction but untested

Evaluates: Multi-language debugging (Go/C), state machine comprehension,
           save/restore logic, fog-of-war algorithms

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move test dependencies from the Dockerfile into test.sh and either add a
  test for one-case.sh or remove its mention from the instructions.
================================================================================

output_data.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction explicitly covers the key behaviors that tests verify: the matrix command (`bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh`) writing `/app/output/resume-audit.json`; the `cases` array containing rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, warden; each case having name/ok/fresh/resume/moves/visibility/effects/records/log; fresh and resume summaries carrying illegal/steps/sig; the clean conditions (ok==true, illegal==0, sig match); records being a JSONL file mirroring the moves in order; log being whitespace-delimited rows in the same order; and `/app/bin/rogctl` being left in place. The instruction also describes the failure modes being fixed (blocking through walls, acting from unseen positions). Minor inconsistency: the instruction calls the actor field "side id" while the JSON uses `actor`, but the agent works with existing typed code and does not define the schema from scratch.
❌ fail - behavior_in_tests: The instruction explicitly specifies: "Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit." There is no test for this behavior — no invocation of one-case.sh, no check that single-<case>.json exists, and no comparison to the matrix audit row. This is a named, specified output with a defined correctness criterion that is entirely absent from the test suite.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Tests are clearly named and each has a one-sentence docstring explaining what is being checked (e.g., "Baseline uninterrupted and restored paths stay clean for rook", "Sentinel timed modifiers must still be active immediately after restore"). Helper functions (assert_case_clean, load_layout, final_player_pos, lit_tiles, step_legal) are well-named and single-purpose. The session-scoped fixture `matrix_data` cleanly separates setup from individual checks. The structure is readable and easy to maintain.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the image, so the agent cannot read the tests or solution at runtime. The test fixture invokes the actual engine binary via subprocess and checks the resulting JSON, JSONL, and TSV files produced by the engine — there is no hardcoded expected output to match against. The solution itself works by patching buggy source files and recompiling, then running the real engine. There is no mutable external network resource (internet is disabled). The engine's correctness is verified end-to-end, not via string matching on pre-existing files.
❌ fail - structured_data_schema: The instruction does not document the exact JSON field names. It calls the actor field "side id" while the actual key is `actor` (used in tests as `move["actor"]`). "From coordinates, to coordinates" does not name the actual keys `from_x`, `from_y`, `to_x`, `to_y` that tests access directly. The `effects` array schema (fields `id`, `active`, `epoch`) is not documented at all, yet `test_effect_epoch_sentinel` accesses `row["id"]` and `ward[0]["active"]`. The log/trace file column layout (case_name\tturn\tactor\tfrom_x\tfrom_y\tto_x\tto_y\tlegal) is not specified — the test relies on column positions `parts[1]`, `parts[2]`, `parts[-1]` without any documented schema. Only descriptive prose ("whitespace-delimited rows") is given.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned by SHA-256 digest. All apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, golang-go=2:1.24~2, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Python packages are version-pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). The Go module has no external dependencies beyond the standard library.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Filenames are consistent throughout (resume-audit.json, single-<case>.json, run-matrix.sh, one-case.sh, rogctl). JSON field names in types, tests, and instruction are internally consistent. Script paths, Go package paths, and make targets all match their references.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies Makefile, go.mod, README.md, cmd/, config/, data/, docs/, internal/, probe/, and scripts/ into /app. Neither tests/ nor solution/ is copied into the image, so the agent cannot access them at runtime.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution patches four source files (b1/cache.go, c2/effect.go, d3/plan.go, probe/legality.c) with corrected implementations, applies targeted sed/Python string replacements to a0/pack.go and f5/run.go to fix specific logic bugs (Seen slice zeroing on pack, wrong resume-path loop structure), then runs `make build` to compile and `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` to produce the output. The solution derives the answer through an actual build-and-run process rather than emitting hardcoded JSON.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: The instruction explicitly names all agent-facing output files: `/app/output/resume-audit.json` (produced by run-matrix.sh) and `/app/output/single-<case>.json` (produced by one-case.sh). The binary `/app/bin/rogctl` is also explicitly mentioned as being left in place. The records JSONL and trace TSV paths are not named in the instruction, but they are embedded inside the audit JSON output (via the `records` and `log` fields) and not files the agent is expected to name directly.

The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.

2026-05-25 15:55:35,548 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (29 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The test suite validates structural consistency and legality of moves but never
asserts that any moves are actually produced, allowing a trivial shortcut that
disables all hostile movement to pass every test.

Strengths:  Tests independently recompute expected visibility from layout files
and cross-reference move legality/coordinates against ground truth; the
sidecar-file tests verify structural alignment between output formats.

Weaknesses: No test asserts moves arrays are non-empty; all move-validation
loops are vacuous when moves is empty; the one-case.sh script described in the
instruction is never tested.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite runs the matrix build, checks that all six cases report ok=true
with matching signatures and zero illegal moves, then spot-checks wall
legality, visibility, effect persistence, and sidecar file alignment. However,
every move-based assertion iterates over case["moves"], which passes trivially
when that list is empty. An agent can disable hostile movement with a one-line
edit to PlanSlot (return false unconditionally), producing empty moves for
both fresh and resume paths, matching signatures, zero illegals, and empty
sidecar files — satisfying all 13 test assertions without fixing the actual
resume-state bug.

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. No minimum-moves assertion enables empty-output shortcut
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:112-136 (assert_case_clean)
Problem: The function validates moves only via a for-loop that never executes
when case["moves"] is an empty list. No assertion requires len(moves) > 0 for
any case. Since every scenario script contains at least one "H go" step that
should produce hostile movement, the tests should require non-empty output.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      if move["kind"] == "step":                                             │
│          assert step_legal(walls, ...)                                      │
│          assert (move["to_x"], move["to_y"]) in visible                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(case["moves"]) > 0, f"{case['name']} produced no moves"         │
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      ...                                                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An agent can make PlanSlot always return false (one-line edit in
internal/d3/plan.go), preventing any hostile from ever moving. Both fresh and
resume paths then produce zero moves, identical empty-list signatures, zero
illegals, and ok=true. All 13 tests pass without fixing the actual resume
state-restoration bugs in a0/pack.go and c2/effect.go.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Sidecar file tests are vacuous on empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:209-233 (test_sidecar_order_lancer,
         test_trace_sidecar_warden)
Problem: Both tests assert len(file_rows) == len(case["moves"]) then zip
over pairs. When both are zero-length, the equality passes and the zip loop
never executes. These tests provide no signal when the shortcut is applied.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) > 0, "records file must contain at least one row"        │
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without a minimum-length guard, an agent that zeroes out all
hostile movement produces empty sidecar files that satisfy len==len and
never enters the validation loop — the test is structurally vacuous.

================================================================================
                          WEAK ASSERTIONS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Signature match check is trivially satisfiable with empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:127, 178
Problem: The sig is computed via FNV-hash over the moves list. Two empty lists
hash to the same initial constant, so sig equality is guaranteed without any
actual move reconciliation occurring.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert case["fresh"]["sig"] == case["resume"]["sig"]                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert case["fresh"]["steps"] > 0                                          │
│  assert case["resume"]["steps"] > 0                                         │
│  assert case["fresh"]["sig"] == case["resume"]["sig"]                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The instruction specifically says "signatures match when both
paths ran opposing turns" — implying non-trivial turn execution. Without
requiring steps > 0, the test degenerates to comparing a constant with itself.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. one-case.sh functionality is completely untested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (no test exists)
Problem: The instruction states "Running bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>
writes /app/output/single-<case>.json with the same top-level cases wrapper
and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit." No test
exercises this script or validates its output against the matrix audit.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test exists)                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_one_case_matches_matrix(matrix_data):                             │
│      for name in CASE_NAMES:                                                │
│          proc = subprocess.run(                                              │
│              ["bash", str(APP / "scripts/one-case.sh"), name], ...)          │
│          single = json.loads(Path(f"/app/output/single-{name}.json")        │
│              .read_text())                                                   │
│          assert single["cases"][0] == case_by_name(matrix_data, name)       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a secondary constraint from the instruction that has no
coverage. An agent could break one-case.sh without detection.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ⚠️  VULNERABLE
Severity:  Critical

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: STRENGTHEN
The test suite validates structural consistency and legality of moves but never
asserts that any moves are actually produced, allowing a trivial shortcut that
disables all hostile movement to pass every test.

Strengths:  Tests independently recompute expected visibility from layout files
and cross-reference move legality/coordinates against ground truth; the
sidecar-file tests verify structural alignment between output formats.

Weaknesses: No test asserts moves arrays are non-empty; all move-validation
loops are vacuous when moves is empty; the one-case.sh script described in the
instruction is never tested.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite runs the matrix build, checks that all six cases report ok=true
with matching signatures and zero illegal moves, then spot-checks wall
legality, visibility, effect persistence, and sidecar file alignment. However,
every move-based assertion iterates over case["moves"], which passes trivially
when that list is empty. An agent can disable hostile movement with a one-line
edit to PlanSlot (return false unconditionally), producing empty moves for
both fresh and resume paths, matching signatures, zero illegals, and empty
sidecar files — satisfying all 13 test assertions without fixing the actual
resume-state bug.

================================================================================
                            CRITICAL GAPS ❌
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. No minimum-moves assertion enables empty-output shortcut
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:112-136 (assert_case_clean)
Problem: The function validates moves only via a for-loop that never executes
when case["moves"] is an empty list. No assertion requires len(moves) > 0 for
any case. Since every scenario script contains at least one "H go" step that
should produce hostile movement, the tests should require non-empty output.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      if move["kind"] == "step":                                             │
│          assert step_legal(walls, ...)                                      │
│          assert (move["to_x"], move["to_y"]) in visible                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(case["moves"]) > 0, f"{case['name']} produced no moves"         │
│  for move in case["moves"]:                                                 │
│      assert move["legal"] is True                                           │
│      ...                                                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An agent can make PlanSlot always return false (one-line edit in
internal/d3/plan.go), preventing any hostile from ever moving. Both fresh and
resume paths then produce zero moves, identical empty-list signatures, zero
illegals, and ok=true. All 13 tests pass without fixing the actual resume
state-restoration bugs in a0/pack.go and c2/effect.go.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Sidecar file tests are vacuous on empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:209-233 (test_sidecar_order_lancer,
         test_trace_sidecar_warden)
Problem: Both tests assert len(file_rows) == len(case["moves"]) then zip
over pairs. When both are zero-length, the equality passes and the zip loop
never executes. These tests provide no signal when the shortcut is applied.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert len(lines) > 0, "records file must contain at least one row"        │
│  assert len(lines) == len(case["moves"])                                    │
│  for left, right in zip(lines, case["moves"]):                              │
│      assert left["turn"] == right["turn"]                                   │
│      assert left["actor"] == right["actor"]                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Without a minimum-length guard, an agent that zeroes out all
hostile movement produces empty sidecar files that satisfy len==len and
never enters the validation loop — the test is structurally vacuous.

================================================================================

================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Signature match check is trivially satisfiable with empty moves
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:127, 178
Problem: The sig is computed via FNV-hash over the moves list. Two empty lists
hash to the same initial constant, so sig equality is guaranteed without any

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert case["fresh"]["sig"] == case["resume"]["sig"]                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
